title: Proactive tail harmonizer and cohort splitter for longitudinal benchmarks
preview:
Context and motivation: Longitudinal benchmark runs at Redwood have shown unstable tail behavior when cohorts drift across multiple phases (warmup -> steady -> degraded). This PR introduces a lightweight 'Proactive Tail Harmonizer' that proactively detects cohort drift and a deterministic 'Cohort Splitter' that partitions sessions into stable cohorts for more reproducible long-tail quantiles. The goal is to reduce p99/p999 variance across repeated runs and make performance changes easier to attribute to code or config changes.

What this PR delivers (summary): 1) An in-process harmonizer module that computes cohort fingerprints over sliding windows and applies a corrective reweighting when cohort composition shifts by >5% for the tail cohort. 2) A deterministic cohort splitter that marks sessions into stable buckets based on request size distribution, KV cache hit patterns, and primer length. 3) A downstream exporter that annotates existing benchmark histograms with cohort tags so downstream analysis can isolate regressions per cohort. 4) Integration test harness + a new smoke profile that reproduces a previously non-deterministic p999 spike locally.

Design notes (high level): - Harmonizer runs as a lightweight sampler on the bench agent; it maintains a compact Bloom-filter style fingerprint for session features (sequence length histogram, KV-hit pattern signature, primer tokens). - When the harmonizer detects a drift, it emits a reweight instruction to the workload generator to over-sample or under-sample specific cohorts for the remainder of the epoch. This is a corrective step (not blocking) to prevent single-cohort dominance. - Cohort Splitter uses deterministic hashing on normalized feature vectors to guarantee repeatability across runs; the mapping is stable unless the feature normalization changes. - Exporter updates the telemetry envelope with `cohort_id` and `cohort_health` fields so existing dashboards can be filtered with minimal changes.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of harmonizer and splitter modules
- [x] Unit tests for fingerprinting and deterministic hashing
- [x] Integration smoke that reproduces flaky p999 (bench-suite/fixtures/p999-flaky.json)
- [x] Telemetry exporter annotations and docs snippet
- [x] CI passing after flaky test rerun

Testing and validation:
- Ran 120 repeated runs against the local mini-cluster; variance in p999 decreased from 28% to 6% across the canonical seq-mix workload. Numbers and raw traces are attached to ENG-892.
- Verified no measurable penalty to median latency (p50 change < 1ms) in steady-state.
- CI initially flagged a flaky integration test (bench-integration::session-replay) — root cause was a nondeterministic seed in the fixture; fixed by aligning seeds in the smoke profile.

Backward compatibility and rollout:
- Harmonizer default is off; feature flag `bench.harmonizer.enabled` is set to false in main. The PR includes a rollout plan to enable in canary runs for one week before broader baseline updates.
- Cohort Splitter is safe to enable by default because mapping is deterministic; warning added to docs about changing normalization.

Related: ENG-892 (investigation), ENG-904 (dashboard followup), docs PR will follow to redwood-docs.

Files touched (high-level):
- bench-agent/harmonizer/harmonizer.go -- core fingerprinting and drift detector
- bench-agent/harmonizer/fingerprint_test.go -- unit tests
- bench-generator/cohort_splitter.go -- deterministic hashing + sampling control hooks
- bench-exporter/cohort_annotations.go -- telemetry annotation logic and serializer
- bench-suite/profiles/p999-smoke.json -- smoke profile that triggers the spike used for validation
- bench-integration/fixtures/p999-flaky.json -- fixed nondeterministic seed
- bench-ci/workflows/harmonizer-smoke.yml -- workflow to run smoke in PRs

Commits (summary):
- init: add harmonizer core and interfaces
- tests: add fingerprint unit tests and fixtures
- feature: implement cohort splitter and generator hooks
- exporter: annotate telemetry with cohort_id and cohort_health
- ci: add smoke workflow and fix seeds in fixture

Change rationale: The lab's previous approach relied on passive post-hoc cohort tagging which made attribution noisy; this PR moves the lab to proactive stabilization so regressions affecting small cohorts don't get masked by run-to-run cohort shifts.

Rollout plan (short): stage -> canary (2 weeks with harmonizer enabled) -> baseline update with cohort-aware histograms -> enable exporter everywhere.

Notes: This PR purposefully keeps harmonizer heuristics conservative; thresholds are feature-flagged for experimentation. Documentation and dashboards updates are tracked in ENG-904.

title: KernelTide contention visualizer and probabilistic warmup coordinator for stability-focused bench suites
preview:
Motivation: We've observed non-deterministic p95/p99 spikes in a subset of long-running throughput benchmarks that correlate with kernel-level contention and uneven warmup behavior across workers. This PR introduces two complementary components: (1) KernelTide — a contention visualizer that aggregates per-kernel occupancy, stall-reasons and scheduling collisions into time-aligned heatmaps and compact contention-curves; (2) ProbabilisticWarmupCoordinator — a controller that runs randomized preflight primer passes across cohorts to probabilistically cover different KV cache and memory-placement states before measurement windows.

What this PR does (summary): Adds a lightweight sampling probe that annotates kernel events in the benchmark worker (Linux perf + runtime hooks), a postprocessor that synthesizes per-request kernel timelines into a compact parquet-friendly schema, an interactive visualizer (static HTML + JS) for heatmaps and contour plots, and a coordinator that schedules warmup primers with configurable coverage confidence and early-stop heuristics. Integrates with existing bench orchestration and the CI regression pipeline so nightly runs produce kernel-contour artifacts and the stable-run coordinator reduces variance for p95/p99 assertions.

Why this is different: Existing tools produce flamegraphs and token-latency histograms, but none expose the time-correlated kernel-contention signal mapped back to sequence phase and KV-cache state. KernelTide surfaces short-lived contention modes that cause ephemeral long-tail spikes. The ProbabilisticWarmupCoordinator reduces false-positive regressions by ensuring a statistically representative warmup set prior to capture windows.

Compatibility and runbook: Backwards compatible with existing bench manifests. To opt-in add "kerneltide: true" to a workload stanza; warmup coordinator config is optional and defaults to conservative settings. New artifacts are stored under artifacts/kerneltide/{run_id} with a _contours.parquet file and an index.json. README includes visualization usage and troubleshooting steps.

Notes on measurement: We ran the stability suite against a 16-node mini-cluster (mixed A100/V100) with synthetic seq-mix and real prompt traces; KernelTide flagged a scheduler thrash on one node that correlated with a p99 increase of ~18%. After applying the coordinator, p99 variance decreased 34% across 30 runs. Full benchmark logs and the reproduce script are attached in the PR files.

Related: ENG-8342 (noise reduction workstream), ENG-8401 (observability exports), SUP-231 (customer incident that prompted deeper per-kernel analysis).

title: phase-shifted-latency-sweep-and-coldstart-prober
preview:
Motivation: We need a lightweight, reproducible runner that exercises both steady-state and phase-shifted burst patterns while simultaneously measuring cold-start latency across different model variants and runtime kernel configs. This PR introduces a phase-shifted latency sweep runner and a paired cold-start prober that can be composed to produce joint distributions (latency vs cold-start tail) used by the regression detector pipeline.
Design:
- phase_sweep_runner: Python runner that generates a sequence of workload phases with configurable duration, RPS, sequence length distribution and an adjustable phase-offset parameter to produce controlled interleaving across client shards.
- coldstart_prober: small Go probe that issues warmup-less single-shot requests on a configurable model selection (quantized/FP16/FP32) to capture cold-start percentiles under concurrent load.
- reporter bridge: new CSV+JSON reporter that tags samples with phase-id, shard-id, kernel-profile and coldstart-epoch for downstream aggregation.
- CI harness changes: bench job integration and a perf-regression threshold test that runs an abbreviated sweep in CI with synthetic small-models.
How to run: bench/README contains examples; typical run: python bench/runners/phase_sweep_runner.py --profile profiles/low_mem.yaml --phases 6 --phase-duration 30s --phase-offset 7s --out reports/run-<ts>.csv
The coldstart probe is compiled with go build and invoked alongside the runner to annotate early-epoch samples.
Implementation notes:
- Uses existing bench client abstraction for request generation and adds a deterministic pseudo-random seed to make runs reproducible.
- Phase IDs are encoded into the request metadata header X-Bench-Phase so runtime traces can correlate tokens to phase.
- Reporter writes a compact JSON lines stream and a summary CSV compatible with the regression detector.
- Adds a small integration test that validates phase interleaving logic and a smoke perf check for the probe binary.
Commits:
- "initial phase_sweep_runner implementation, core API and simple CLI"
- "add coldstart_prober (go) and CI smoke job"
- "reporter: csv + jsonlines bridge and header tagging"
- "docs: bench user guide and example profiles"
- "fix: deterministic seeding + durable temp file cleanup"
Files changed (high level):
- bench/runners/phase_sweep_runner.py (new): core runner, phase scheduler, sampling hooks
- bench/probers/coldstart_prober/main.go (new): standalone probe binary
- bench/reporters/csv_json_bridge.go (new): reporter bridge for CSV/JSONL outputs
- bench/configs/profiles/phase_sweep_low_mem.yaml (new): example profile
- integration/test_phase_sweep.py (new): lightweight unit/integration checks
- docs/bench/phase-sweep.md (new): usage and interpretation
- ci/bench.yml (modified): adds perf sweep smoke job
Reviewers requested small API changes (explicit --seed flag, clearer exit codes for the probe) and asked for a more conservative default for phase-offset to avoid self-synchronization artifacts on noisy CI hosts. I iterated on the runner CLI, added --seed and made the default phase-offset randomized within a small jitter when running in CI mode.
Tests: unit tests for scheduler logic and an integration runner simulation were added.
CI summary:
- build: pass
- unit-tests: pass
- integration-tests: pass
- bench-smoke: pass (flaky on first run; retried once)
- perf-regression-check: pass (abbreviated)
Merge: squashed and merged to main.
Notes on impact and usage:
- This enables the benchmark lab to capture correlated latency tailing behavior during workload phase transitions and quantify cold-start exposure for short-lived containers.
- New CSV schema is backwards-compatible with the regression detector after a small mapping layer; a follow-up will add direct ingestion.
Checklist:
- [x] Runner implementation
- [x] Coldstart probe
- [x] Reporter bridge and CSV schema
- [x] CI integration
- [x] Docs and examples
- [x] Tests and smoke checks
Related: ENG-4821 tracks adding cold-start monitoring to the nightly suites; ENG-4890 is a follow-up to integrate these runs into the SLA dashboard.

title: Add GPU thermal-throttle emulator and warmup stability harness
preview:
Motivation: Intermittent production incidents traced to GPU thermal/power throttling during long inference bursts caused rare regressions in tail latency and kernel scheduling. We need a reproducible, low-overhead emulator to inject thermal/power profiles into benchmark runs and a repeatable warmup-stability harness to measure time-to-steady-state under those conditions. This PR introduces a GPU thermal-throttle emulator and a warmup stability harness that: 1) simulates frequency/power capping curves, 2) injects transient throttles at configurable epochs, 3) coordinates with the existing warmup priming runner to capture time-to-convergence and tail variance, and 4) writes compact stability artifacts for downstream analysis.

What changed (high level): - New thermal_emulator/ module (Python) that runs an in-process state machine describing temperature/power curves and emits a small shim library to throttle CUDA kernel launch pacing via a token-bucket backpressure hook. - warmup_harness/ runner that composes the emulator with multi-sequence primer scenarios and records per-epoch quantiles, KV cache hit rates, and kernel scheduling counters. - reports/stability exporter that generates a single 'stabscore' JSON and a zipped set of perf flamepoints for each run. - CI bench job orchestration added to ci/bench_ci.yaml with a soft-failure rerun for flaky perf thresholds. - docs/thermal_emulator.md with runbook and failure-mode guidance.

Why this is different: Existing harnesses focus on request pattern and KV cache priming; they don't model hardware-level throttles. The emulator is intentionally lightweight (no privileged operations) — it simulates the observable effects (reduced effective throughput, increased per-kernel latency variance) so we can reproduce outages in CI without special infra.

How to run locally: 1) scripts/setup_bench_env.sh --install-deps 2) python -m benchmark_lab.thermal_emulator --profile profiles/cooldown.json & 3) ./scripts/run_warmup_harness.sh --config examples/warmup/heavy_gpu_p95.yaml --output /tmp/stability_run.zip

Checklist: - [x] Implementation of emulator core and token-bucket shim - [x] Warmup harness integration and reporters - [x] Unit tests for emulator state transitions - [x] Small-scale system test run on internal GPU queue (passed) - [x] Documentation and runbook - [x] Bench CI job added (soft-failure enabled)

Performance summary (representative): On the 'heavy_gpu_p95' scenario we saw: baseline stabscore 0.94 -> under simulated throttle 0.72 (regression captured), median latency increase 18%, p99 increase 42%, KV cache priming time increased by 1.7x. Flamepoints attached to report for kernel-level attribution.

Migration notes: No customer-facing API changes. The emulator is opt-in for benchmark runs and disabled by default in nightly pipelines. Recommend adding a dedicated periodic CI job (weekly) that runs the harness against the production model catalog to detect regressions related to thermal sensitivity.

Related work: ENG-2145 (investigate tail incidents), ENG-3301 (warmup priming improvements).

title: Introduce latency-distribution-collector and histogram-exporter for perf runs
preview: Motivation: our current perf suite reports mean/median p50/p95/p99 but lacks a first-class histogram distribution export that lets ops and product slices examine tail behavior by bucket and request class. This PR introduces a latency-distribution-collector (benchmarks/runner/latency_collector.py) and a histogram_exporter that emits fixed-window histograms and a Prometheus-compatible scrape endpoint for aggregated CI runs. Key changes: 1) adds a thread-safe latency_collector that supports labeled buckets (model, route, sequence_length_bin) and controlled flush intervals; 2) implements histogram_exporter with configurable bucket boundaries and JSON/Prometheus output modes; 3) integrates collection step into ci/perf-pipeline.yml so nightly jobs persist artifacts to the perf artifacts store; 4) adds validation tests and an examples script to reproduce locally. Implementation notes: the collector uses a ring buffer to bound memory and an optimized bucket increment path to minimize overhead during tight throughput tests; the exporter supports on-the-fly delta exports to reduce payload size for long runs. Checklist: - [x] design and prototype latency collector; - [x] unit tests covering concurrency and bucket edge cases; - [x] exporter with both JSON and Prometheus formats; - [x] CI integration and artifact upload; - [x] docs and example runbook. Backwards compatibility: output formats default to JSON with the old summary generator still available; no changes to existing benchmark input schemas. Perf: measured <1% overhead at 2k rps in synthetic harness; added ability to capture per-route tail behavior that was previously invisible. How to validate: run examples/bench/collect_and_export.sh against a local runner to produce artifacts and then run docs/benchmarks/latency-distribution.md validation steps which include checksum and bucket-sanity assertions. Related work: follows on ENG-4012 (tail-analysis initiative) and pairs with the perf-viewer dashboard from ENG-3987. Security: exporter binds only to loopback by default and CI uploads go to the internal artifacts bucket with ACLs. Release notes: Adds latency distribution collector and histogram exporter to benchmark lab enabling tail analysis and Prometheus-compatible exports. Breaking change: false.

title: introduce kernel-variance prober to map per-layer sequence-length inefficiencies
preview: Motivation: We frequently see tail latency regressions that only appear for particular sequence-length distributions. Existing workload runners produce aggregate token-level metrics but do not attribute variance to model kernels or layers. This PR adds a 'kernel-variance prober' that runs focused microbenchmarks across sequence length windows and collects fine-grained per-kernel timing and variance profiles so we can prioritize kernel/graph-level optimizations and targeted quantization tradeoffs. Change summary: - New binary tools/bench/prober/kernel_variance_prober.py: orchestrates probing campaigns across sequence-length buckets, random seeds, and batch shapes; supports warmup/steady-state detection and adaptive sampling. - Benchmark harness extensions bench/harness/sequence_sampler.py: sequence-length aware samplers and an API to plug sampling policies into existing runners. - Profiling exporter bench/reporters/kernel_variance_reporter.go: aggregates raw kernel timing logs into per-layer heatmaps and high-variance segment reports; outputs ndjson for downstream ingestion. - Integration test bench/tests/test_kernel_variance_endtoend.py: small runtime smoke verifying consistent profiling across CPU/GPU backends. - CI job .github/workflows/benchmark-prober.yml: a lightweight workflow that runs the prober in a constrained emulation mode for PRs to prevent regression in core loops. Why this is different: instead of only measuring latency or throughput aggregates, the prober attributes variance to kernel implementations (attention, rotary, layernorm, matmul) and to sequence-length regions which helps routing, batching and kernel-selection decisions in the serving runtime. Usage notes: the prober can be invoked locally with `tools/prober/run_prober --profile-config examples/probes/length_scan.yaml`; output is an ndjson stream consumable by the reporting pipeline. Backwards compatibility: no runtime or serving changes; this is a benchmark-only change. Rollout plan: land as experimental tooling behind a feature flag; run nightly full-resolution campaigns for critical models and surface regressions in the benchmark dashboard.

title: Epochal hotpath bisector and phase-stress harness for p95 reproduction
preview:
Motivation: We've seen recurring intermittent p95 regressions across long-running multi-epoch workloads where the signal only appears after many warmup cycles. Root-cause is often a narrow commit window interacting with kernel scheduler behaviour and warmup timing. This PR introduces two complementary tools: (1) an epochal hotpath bisector that runs compact workload epochs across commit ranges to quickly narrow offending commits, and (2) a phase-stress harness that injects controlled scheduling/jitter/prefetch delays into the runtime to reliably amplify the rare p95 event so it becomes reproducible in CI/local benches.

What this change does: Implements a bisector CLI that (a) shards an epochal workload into deterministic micro-epochs, (b) runs them across a git commit range with parallel workers and conservative resource isolation, and (c) aggregates per-epoch phase-bucket histograms to highlight commit-correlated shifts. Adds a phase-stress harness that can shim the runtime with configurable preemption latency, simulated kernel prefetch stalls, and induced KV-cache fragmentation to stress the hotpath under controlled conditions. Also adds a compact serializer (phase-bucket format) and a small reporting pipeline that generates a delta summary highlighting shifted quantiles and hotpath stack tags.

How to run (short): 1) Install bench Docker image: docker build -f Dockerfile.bench -t rwl-bench:phase . 2) Run bisector: bench/bisect --start <commit> --end <commit> --epochs 50 --workers 6 --mode phase-stress --stress-config bench/phase_stress/config.yaml 3) Inspect reports in benchmarks/reporting/<run-id>.phase.json and open docs/benchmarking/hotpath-bisector.md for guidance.

Checklist: - [x] Bisector core CLI, control plane, and parallel runner - [x] Phase-stress harness with 3 stress primitives (preemption, prefetch stall, kv-frag) - [x] Phase-bucket serializer and delta reporter - [x] Unit tests for serializer and small integration test for harness - [x] CI workflow to run a fast smoke bench on PRs - [x] Docs + example primer JSON - [ ] Long-run nightly job wiring (follow-up ENG-1708)

Testing and results: Local reproduction: used a historical regression window and reduced the commit range from ~2k commits to a 6-commit candidate using the bisector in ~45 minutes (6 workers, 50 epochs). Phase-stress amplified the original intermittent p95 from ~850ms to a stable 1.4–1.8s under the stress profile used in the reproduction (config included). CI: fast smoke run in PR pipeline completes within ~12 minutes (benchmarks.yml) and all checks passed after two re-runs on flaky network-injected step.

Limitations and risk: This is experimental and intentionally feature-flagged. The phase-stress primitives are intrusive (they modify scheduling hooks in the bench runtime) and can perturb other telemetry; their use should be limited to debug runs. Bisector increases CI compute usage when run across large commit ranges; the default parallelism is conservative.

Rollout plan: merge gated under label "experimental" and enable for internal SRE+perf teams. Nightly long-run job wiring and a small Linear task to auto-create candidate PRs when a bisect run identifies a single offending commit is tracked under ENG-1708.

Follow-ups: (1) Add automatic regression-gist generation for use in alert tickets (ENG-1642), (2) add an option to replay bisector rundata against dedicated GPU pools, (3) improve phase-bucket visualization in Console.

Contact: ava@redwood.example for bisector CLI questions and miguel@redwood.example for harness details.

title: Add quant profile rollback benchmarks (latency + quality) and CI artifact export
preview:
Title: Add quant profile rollback benchmarks (latency + quality) and CI artifact export

Context
We’ve had a few regressions during quant profile experiments where we needed to confirm that a rollback actually restores baseline behavior (both performance and quality) before unpausing a rollout. This PR adds first-class benchmark scenarios to benchmark-lab that (1) compare two quant profile versions head-to-head and (2) validate that a rollback from candidate -> baseline produces results within defined tolerances.

What’s in this PR
- New benchmark suite: quant_profile_rollback
  - Runs A/B comparisons for two quant profile versions (e.g., qp_v12 vs qp_v11) on the same prompt distribution and concurrency.
  - Runs an explicit rollback scenario: baseline -> candidate -> baseline, ensuring the second baseline run matches the first baseline run (within noise thresholds).
- Metrics captured
  - Perf: p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency, tokens/sec, time-to-first-token, GPU util (if exported by runner), request 5xx rate.
  - Quality: task-level aggregate metrics from the eval harness output (pass@1 / exact match where applicable), plus a lightweight “output drift” metric (normalized Levenshtein distance over a fixed deterministic subset) to detect surprising changes that still pass task thresholds.
- Artifacts
  - Emits a single JSON summary artifact suitable for safe-apply gates: artifacts/quant_profile_rollback_summary.json.
  - Includes tags for tenant/model/region/config_version when provided (for parity with the rollout health dashboard annotations).
- CI integration
  - Adds a CPU-only “smoke” mode for PR validation (schema + harness wiring + artifact format).
  - Keeps the full GPU run behind the existing nightly / on-demand workflow (no new default GPU CI spend).

How it works
- The suite takes two quant profiles and a model target:
  - --baseline-profile (required)
  - --candidate-profile (required)
  - --model (default: rw/llama-3.1-8b-instruct)
- It executes three phases in order:
  1) baseline: run prompts, record perf + quality, save output fingerprints for deterministic subset
  2) candidate: same workload, record deltas vs baseline
  3) rollback_to_baseline: re-run baseline profile and compare against phase (1)
- Pass/fail logic is intentionally conservative and mirrors the gating ADR direction:
  - rollback baseline perf must be within +3% p95 and +5% p99 latency vs initial baseline (configurable)
  - rollback baseline quality must be within 0.2% absolute of initial baseline on aggregate metric (configurable)
  - output drift on deterministic subset must not exceed threshold (defaults tuned to avoid flaking)

Usage
- Local (requires access to benchmark runner + dataset):
  - ./bench run quant_profile_rollback --model rw/llama-3.1-8b-instruct --baseline-profile qp_v11 --candidate-profile qp_v12 --concurrency 32 --prompt-set prompts/quant_canary_v3.jsonl
- CI (smoke):
  - .github/workflows/bench-smoke.yml runs with --smoke and validates summary artifact schema.
- Nightly/on-demand GPU:
  - .github/workflows/bench-quant-rollback.yml

Files / areas changed (high level)
- benches/quant_profile_rollback/*: new scenario + runners
- harness/quality/*: adapter for pulling aggregate metrics + deterministic subset drift calculation
- tools/artifacts/export_summary.py: JSON summary writer shared with other suites
- .github/workflows/bench-smoke.yml: schema smoke
- .github/workflows/bench-quant-rollback.yml: nightly/on-demand GPU execution
- docs/benchmarks/quant_profile_rollback.md: how to run + interpret output

Illustrative summary JSON (excerpt)
{
  "suite": "quant_profile_rollback",
  "model": "rw/llama-3.1-8b-instruct",
  "baseline_profile": "qp_v11",
  "candidate_profile": "qp_v12",
  "baseline": {"p95_ms": 182.4, "p99_ms": 311.7, "quality": 0.8421},
  "candidate": {"p95_ms": 175.1, "p99_ms": 298.2, "quality": 0.8416},
  "rollback_to_baseline": {"p95_ms": 183.6, "p99_ms": 314.1, "quality": 0.8420},
  "gates": {"rollback_perf_ok": true, "rollback_quality_ok": true, "drift_ok": true}
}

Notes / limitations
- This does not attempt to prove kernel-level cache invalidation correctness. It is an end-to-end benchmark meant to catch “mixed state” issues and obvious regressions after profile swaps.
- The drift metric is only evaluated on a deterministic subset (fixed seed, temp=0, top_p=1) to keep noise low.

Review discussion (summary)
- Jonas Weber: Requested that quality gate be absolute-delta, not relative, and that we record the exact eval harness version/hash in the summary.
  - Arjun Desai: Updated gates to absolute delta, added harness_git_sha in artifact metadata.
- Sana Farid: Flagged potential flake from small sample size on the deterministic subset; suggested increasing subset from 64 -> 128 and adding a minimum token count.
  - Arjun Desai: Bumped subset to 128, added min_total_tokens guard; smoke mode uses a smaller subset but only validates schema.
- Noah Patel: Asked whether rollback run should warm up separately to avoid cold-start skew.
  - Arjun Desai: Added warmup_requests per phase (default 20) and excluded warmup from metrics.
- Sean Gallagher: Asked for clear “operator reading” in docs and a single red/green output line suitable for runbooks.
  - Arjun Desai: Added docs section “What to look at during rollback” and an explicit SUMMARY: PASS/FAIL line.

CI
- bench-smoke (ubuntu-latest): PASS
- lint/format: PASS
- unit tests (harness + artifact schema): PASS
- bench-quant-rollback (on-demand GPU): PASS (attached artifact)

Merge
Squash merged. Follow-up: wire this artifact into perf-canary gate publishing (tracked separately under rollout controller integration work).

title: Generate perf-canary baselines from controlled prod-like load runs
preview: Summary: This PR adds a repeatable baseline generator job in benchmark-lab that computes per-model performance baseline distributions from a controlled prod-like load run. The output artifacts are written in a stable, versioned format that perf-canary can consume for regression evaluation (min samples + percent-change thresholds). Motivation: perf-canary needs a durable baseline per (model, model_version, runtime_build, hardware_sku, region) that reflects real scheduler behavior (queueing/batching) without using customer traffic. Early experiments were using ad-hoc queries on ephemeral metrics, which made it hard to reproduce baselines and triggered baseline drift/cardinality issues during rollout. This job makes baselines an explicit artifact with an audit trail and a consistent schema. Scope / What’s included: (1) New job `baselinegen` that ingests a benchmark-lab load-run result bundle (request-level summary events + runtime counters) and computes: end-to-end latency histograms (p50/p95/p99 + full histogram buckets), runtime-only latency histograms, prefill and decode latency histograms, queueing time histogram, tokens/sec distribution, batch size distribution, and optional KV cache hit-rate summary when present. (2) Baseline artifact format: a single JSON artifact per dimension key, plus an index manifest for discovery. Artifacts are content-addressed and include schema versioning so perf-canary can validate compatibility. (3) Versioning & determinism: the job pins bucket boundaries, uses a seed for any sampling, and stores the exact input run id + git sha + job config in the metadata header. (4) Output location: writes to object storage under `s3://redwood-perf-baselines/perf_canary/v1/` (prod) and `s3://redwood-perf-baselines-staging/perf_canary/v1/` (staging). Includes a thin abstraction so storage backend can be swapped later without touching canary. (5) Guardrails: hard cap on dimensions written per execution (default 200) to prevent accidental wide fanout; rejects any input that contains raw prompts or request payloads (asserts only shape metrics and aggregated counters). (6) Documentation: adds `docs/perf_canary_baselines.md` describing schema, dimension keys, and how to generate/validate a baseline for a new model or hardware SKU. Non-goals: This does not change perf-canary evaluation logic or alert thresholds (handled in perf-canary repo / ADR). This does not add customer-traffic shadowing. Implementation notes: Input contract expects a `loadrun.jsonl.zst` (one line per request summary) and `runtime_metrics.pb.zst` (aggregated runtime counters) produced by existing benchmark-lab load runner. For histograms we use fixed bucket edges aligned with existing observability-pack dashboards to avoid mismatch between baseline and live metrics. The artifact JSON is intentionally flat and explicit (no nested structures beyond a small header), to keep it easy to diff/review and to allow simple validation in the canary service. Example artifact key: `model=llama-3.1-70b-instruct|model_version=2026-01-10|runtime_build=redwood-1.42.0|kernel_set=flashattn-v3|hardware_sku=h100-sxm|region=us-east-1|tier=hosted`. Testing: Unit tests cover histogram generation, percentile extraction, bucket edge stability, manifest writing, and schema validation. Integration test runs baselinegen on a small fixture loadrun bundle and asserts stable output (golden files). Added a dry-run mode that prints the computed dimension keys and sample counts without writing artifacts. Rollout plan: (1) Run job nightly in staging for the initial canary model set (2-3 models) and compare variance vs current ad-hoc baselines. (2) After sign-off from Runtime + Telemetry, enable in prod with a manual trigger path (run-id passed explicitly) before moving to scheduled runs. (3) Wire perf-canary baseline fetch to the manifest index (separate PR already in progress in perf-canary repo). Links: Linear ENG-4874 (baseline store + versioning), ENG-4872 (perf-canary MVP), ENG-4873 (metrics definitions). Notes for reviewers: Please sanity-check the dimension key policy (especially `kernel_set` and `runtime_build`), histogram bucket edges, and the cardinality guardrails. Also confirm the artifact header contains enough info for audit/debug but not enough to leak sensitive data.

title: Embeddings: add high-QPS mode + throughput-first report + percentile latency export
preview:
Summary: Adds an embeddings workload mode designed for high-QPS / small-payload traffic shapes and a throughput-first reporting path (req/s + embeddings/s, plus tokens/s where applicable). Also exports percentile latency (p50/p95/p99) in machine-readable form for perf-canary + dashboard ingestion. This is needed for Q1 2026 baseline sweeps because embeddings traffic is dominated by concurrency/queueing behavior and req/s saturation, and the existing generator/reporting was text-generation-centric (TTFT/TPOT) and didn’t provide stable throughput/saturation indicators.

Motivation / context: Our representative workload suite v1 includes an embeddings cohort with production-like payload sizes and concurrency targets (high QPS, small input lengths, mostly non-streaming). For baseline comparability we need: (1) a generator that can drive stable, controlled offered load (open-loop) while still supporting closed-loop for latency-first runs, (2) a report that prioritizes throughput and saturation detection (req/s, embeddings/s, error rate, queue wait) over TTFT/TPOT, and (3) percentile export in the canonical results schema (ADR-0172) so SRE/obs can build consistent dashboards and gating thresholds.

What’s in this PR (high level): 1) New workload type: `embeddings_high_qps` - Adds a workload config + generator that issues embeddings requests with configurable input length distributions and concurrency/QPS targets. - Supports both "closed-loop" (N concurrent workers, measure latency) and "open-loop" (target QPS, measure achieved throughput + latency under load). - Adds a warmup stage and steady-state windows to reduce variance (warmup defaults to 60s; measurement defaults to 10m; both configurable).

2) Throughput-first report: `ThroughputReport` - Outputs throughput as: - `requests_per_second` (primary) - `embeddings_per_second` (alias of rps; kept for readability in baseline tables) - `input_tokens_per_second` (optional; only when tokenizer/model metadata is available) - Adds saturation signals: in-flight, queue depth (if provided by server metrics), and client-side backpressure counters.

3) Percentile latency export: - Computes latency percentiles over the steady-state window and writes: - JSON results artifact (`results.json`) aligned with the run manifest id - CSV summary (`summary.csv`) for quick spreadsheet comparisons - Percentiles included: p50/p90/p95/p99 plus min/max; units in milliseconds; explicit measurement boundaries (client wall clock from request send to response fully received).

4) Results schema alignment + tagging: - Results include the same top-level tags as other workloads: model, region, sku, runtime_build, workload_name, seed, and scenario (cold vs warm cache). - Adds `workload_mode=open_loop|closed_loop` and `target_qps` to allow apples-to-apples comparisons and gating.

CLI / usage: - New flags (all optional; defaults tuned for baseline runs): - `--workload embeddings_high_qps` - `--mode open_loop|closed_loop` (default: open_loop) - `--target-qps <float>` (open_loop only) - `--concurrency <int>` (closed_loop only) - `--input-len-dist <preset|path>` (default: preset "prod_p50_small") - `--warmup-seconds <int>` (default: 60) - `--measurement-seconds <int>` (default: 600) - `--export-results-json <path>` and `--export-summary-csv <path>`

Notes on tokens/s: For embeddings, “tokens/s” is ambiguous and easy to misuse (no decode phase; tokenizer choice affects counts). This PR reports `input_tokens_per_second` only when: (a) the model config includes a tokenizer id, (b) tokenization is enabled for the run, and (c) the tokenization version is captured in the run manifest. Otherwise the report emits `input_tokens_per_second=null` and logs a one-line note. The primary baseline metric for embeddings remains req/s + latency percentiles.

Testing / validation: - Added unit tests for: - percentile computation (HDR histogram rounding behavior; verifies p99) - open-loop scheduler stability (target QPS within ±2% in deterministic mode) - schema validation: required fields present; rejects missing tags for baseline runs - Added an e2e smoke test behind CI label `gpu-optional` that runs against the mock server and ensures JSON/CSV artifacts are created.

Changed areas (files): - `cmd/bench/main.py`: adds embeddings workload wiring + flags - `bench/workloads/embeddings_high_qps.py`: new generator + pacing strategies - `bench/reporting/throughput_report.py`: new report + exporters - `bench/results/schema.py`: adds optional throughput fields + `workload_mode` tag - `bench/tests/test_percentiles.py`: percentile export correctness - `docs/workloads/embeddings.md`: new docs section and recommended baseline settings

Example output (trimmed): - `summary.csv` (steady-state): `workload,mode,target_qps,achieved_rps,p50_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms,error_rate` -> `embeddings_high_qps,open_loop,1500,1492.6,18.4,32.1,57.8,0.0003`

Review notes / decisions: - Measurement boundary: client wall-clock (send -> full response received). This matches other benchmark-lab workloads and is consistent with our canary comparisons; we explicitly do NOT attempt to subtract client overhead beyond standard connection reuse. - Open-loop default: chosen for embeddings because it surfaces saturation and avoids hiding queueing by throttling at concurrency limits; closed-loop still supported for latency-only investigations.

Follow-ups (not in scope): - Hook in optional server-side queue wait metric collection from runtime instrumentation (ENG-4826) once it lands in the runtime and is plumbed into benchmark-lab collectors. - Add per-request size tagging to quantify payload-size impact under mixed distributions.

Checklist: - [x] Linked Linear (ENG-4825, ENG-4822) - [x] Docs updated - [x] Results JSON validated against schema - [x] Unit tests added - [x] CI green - [x] Added example baseline config file

Commits (squash summary): - Add embeddings_high_qps workload generator with open-loop pacing - Add throughput-first reporting + JSON/CSV exporters - Align results with manifest tagging + schema updates - Tests + docs

CI: - benchmark-lab / unit-tests: PASS - benchmark-lab / lint: PASS - benchmark-lab / schema-validate: PASS - benchmark-lab / e2e-mockserver: PASS - benchmark-lab / gpu-optional-smoke: SKIPPED on PR (ran manually, attached logs)

Review conversation (highlights): - Claire Dubois: "Can we make it explicit that percentiles are steady-state only (excluding warmup)? Also please add p90." -> Rina: Added `window=steady_state` tag and added p90 to exports; docs now state warmup exclusion. - Sana Farid: "Open-loop pacing: do we have guardrails so we don’t claim throughput when we’re actually dropping/429’ing?" -> Rina: Added error_rate to the report and a `throughput_effective_rps` field (achieved_rps * (1 - error_rate)) used in gating recommendations; also added a hard warning when error_rate > 0.5%. - Sean Gallagher: "CSV is helpful but dashboards want JSON; ensure stable field names and units." -> Rina: Confirmed ms units everywhere, added `*_ms` suffix fields, and pinned schema version in results artifact. - Jonas Weber: "Please add a note about tokenizer versioning so quality/perf analyses don’t get confused." -> Rina: Added `tokenizer_id` and `tokenizer_version` to manifest requirements for token reporting; tokens/s is omitted if not present.

Outcome: Merged (squash). This unblocks embeddings baseline sweeps and perf-canary ingestion for the embeddings cohort.

title: Add long-context prefill workload + standardized warmup to reduce variance
preview:
Title: Add long-context prefill workload + standardized warmup to reduce variance

Context
We need a production-representative workload that stresses prefill (large prompt / long context) with modest decode, and we need more repeatable runs for the Q1 2026 baseline sweeps. In early dry runs we saw high variance driven by (a) first-run JIT + memory pool ramp, (b) GPU clocks/ramp state, and (c) caching artifacts depending on prior suite order. This PR introduces (1) a long-context prefill workload generator and (2) a standardized warmup phase that is applied consistently across workloads (opt-in in config, default-on for long_context_prefill).

What’s in this PR
1) New workload: long_context_prefill
- Goal: mimic long-context summarization / doc QA traffic: large prefill, smaller decode.
- Shape:
  - prompt length bins (tokens): 8k / 16k / 32k (configurable; default mix 0.45/0.45/0.10)
  - max_output_tokens: 256 (default; configurable)
  - streaming: off by default for this workload (we want clean TTFT + TPOT without client read pacing effects)
  - concurrency: target 1/2/4/8 ramps (default 4)
  - request inter-arrival: closed-loop (per-worker) with optional think-time jitter
- Metrics exported (same schema as suite): p50/p95/p99 TTFT, prefill_ms (server-side), decode_tpot_ms, tokens/sec, req/sec, and saturation hints (queue_wait_ms where available).
- Notes:
  - Adds explicit tracking for “prefill-only time” vs end-to-first-token (TTFT). TTFT remains canonical, but prefill_ms is exported for attribution when decode is small.

2) Standardized warmup procedure
- Adds warmup config to harness:
  - warmup.enabled (default true when workload.long_context_prefill is in the run)
  - warmup.iterations_per_concurrency (default 3)
  - warmup.discard_first (default true)
  - warmup.mode: "allocator+jit" (default), "full" (includes a short sustained run to stabilize clocks), or "none"
  - warmup.reset_kv_cache (default true) and warmup.reset_prefix_cache (default true)
- The warmup phase runs before measurement for each (model, region, sku, workload) tuple and for each concurrency level, so we don’t accidentally compare a “cold” c=1 run against a “warm” c=8 run.
- Output artifacts now include a warmup_summary.json so we can debug whether warmup actually executed (and how long it took).

3) Docs + examples
- New doc: docs/workloads/long_context_prefill.md
- Updated: docs/warmup.md to describe modes and the rationale (variance reduction + consistent measurement boundary).
- Added an example run config and sample output under examples/.

Example
Command:
  benchlab run --config configs/suite/long_context_prefill_default.yaml --model llama-3.1-70b-instruct --region us-east --gpu h100 --out artifacts/2026-01-10_longprefill_llama70b_h100_use1

Sample (trimmed) result row:
  workload=long_context_prefill concurrency=4 ttft_p50_ms=182 ttft_p99_ms=411 prefill_p50_ms=148 decode_tpot_p50_ms=33 tokens_per_sec=1080

Why this approach
- This workload captures the real tail-latency pain point we see in production cohorts dominated by large prefills: attention prefill throughput + KV cache pressure + queueing/backpressure behavior.
- Warmup standardizes the “starting state” enough to tighten confidence intervals for regression gating, without hiding real regressions (warmup is consistent across baseline and candidate builds; measured window remains the same).

Files/areas changed (high level)
- workloads/long_context_prefill.py: generator + token length sampling + closed-loop driver
- harness/warmup.py: warmup orchestration and summary artifact
- harness/run.py: integrates warmup into run graph and ensures manifests record warmup settings
- configs/suite/long_context_prefill_default.yaml: default mix + concurrency ramps
- docs/workloads/long_context_prefill.md
- docs/warmup.md
- examples/long_context_prefill/: sample config + a small golden JSON for schema sanity

Checklist
- [x] Adds workload definition + config
- [x] Exports metrics to standard results schema
- [x] Captures warmup config in run manifest
- [x] Adds example run + docs
- [x] CI updated with a short-run smoke test (reduced lengths) to keep runtime reasonable

Open questions / follow-ups
- We may want to add an optional “streaming prefill” variant later (prefill heavy + stream enabled) to measure first-token-on-wire separately. Out of scope for v1 baseline.
- If SRE wants additional fleet-stability knobs (clock locking) we can document those as recommended host settings, but we should avoid baking them into harness logic.

Review conversation (highlights)
Claire Dubois (requested changes): Please be explicit in docs about measurement boundaries: TTFT is end-to-first-token from server perspective; prefill_ms is model-side prefill measurement from inside the server, so it should not be compared to client-observed TTFT. Also add a note on why streaming is off by default.
Rina Kobayashi (author): Done. Updated docs/workloads/long_context_prefill.md with a “Metrics and boundaries” section and clarified streaming default rationale.

Sana Farid: Warmup can mask regressions if it changes caching behavior. Can we guarantee we clear any prefix/KV caches before measurement and record the mode in the manifest?
Rina Kobayashi: Added warmup.reset_kv_cache + warmup.reset_prefix_cache (default true) and included warmup.* fields in the manifest; also wrote warmup_summary.json to make execution auditable.

Sean Gallagher: CI concern: the new workload can be expensive. Please add a smoke test shape in CI (e.g., 2k prompt / 32 output) and gate only on schema + basic execution.
Rina Kobayashi: Added workloads/long_context_prefill_smoke.yaml and wired into ci/perf_smoke.sh; it runs <4 minutes on CI runner GPUs.

Jonas Weber (approval): Looks good. The prompt-length bins match the latest telemetry bins we discussed, and the warmup makes the noise model work simpler. Approved once CI is green.

CI
- benchmark-lab / unit-tests: pass
- benchmark-lab / lint: pass
- benchmark-lab / perf-smoke (short long_context_prefill): pass
- benchmark-lab / schema-validate: pass

Timeline
- 2026-01-07: PR opened
- 2026-01-10: Warmup manifest + cache reset changes added after review
- 2026-01-13: CI green, approved, squashed and merged

Merge
Merged via squash into main. Commit message: "Add long-context prefill workload and standardized warmup". This PR is expected to be used in the first baseline sweeps for ENG-4824 starting this week.

title: Implement chat streaming workload generator (request mix, concurrency ramp, token pacing) + TTFT/TPOT export
preview:
Motivation: We need a production-representative chat streaming workload for the Q1 2026 perf baseline & suite. Existing harness coverage is request/response (non-streaming) only, which under-measures scheduling + queueing dynamics and makes TTFT/TPOT comparisons noisy across client implementations.

What this adds: (1) A chat streaming workload generator (server SSE / chunked streaming) with configurable request-mix, sequence-length bins, and streaming cadence; (2) a concurrency ramp runner (warmup + stepped load) to mimic bursty traffic and reduce variance; (3) standardized baseline outputs for TTFT + TPOT with explicit measurement boundaries (client-side network excluded as much as practical).

Key behaviors: Request mix is modeled as weighted "cohorts" (e.g., short prompt + short decode, short prompt + long decode, medium prompt + medium decode) with configurable distributions. Concurrency ramps from N0 -> N1 -> ... with per-step dwell time, plus a warmup phase that primes model weights and KV allocations without counting metrics. Streaming pacing is implemented as a token-consumption loop that intentionally reads from the stream as fast as possible (to avoid client backpressure becoming the bottleneck). We also capture a separate "client_read_lag_ms" diagnostic to detect accidental client throttling.

Metrics: We compute TTFT as time from request send -> first token/chunk observed by client (first content token, not headers). TPOT is computed as (time from first token -> last token) / (num_tokens_after_first) and exported along with total tokens, completion time, and basic HTTP error classification. Output is emitted as JSONL (one line per request) plus an aggregated summary JSON with p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT, and throughput (req/s and output_tokens/s). The summary file is shaped to be ingested by the baseline dashboards and aligns with ADR-0172 metrics naming (full manifest wiring comes in PR-21432).

How to run: `bench run chat_streaming --endpoint $URL --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east --mix mixes/chat_streaming_v1.json --ramp ramps/step_10min.json --seed 42 --out out/2026-01-05/`.

Notable implementation details: The generator supports both OpenAI-compatible chat completions streaming and Redwood API streaming, controlled via a `protocol` flag. We use a monotonic clock source for timing and include per-request attempt IDs for correlation with server traces. Error handling distinguishes connect timeouts, first-byte timeouts, mid-stream disconnects, and server 429/5xx to help triage overload vs regressions.

Testing: Adds unit tests for distribution sampling and pacing edge cases (empty stream, immediate EOS, chunk coalescing) and a small integration test that replays a canned streaming trace to verify TTFT/TPOT computations are stable.

text:
Motivation: We need a production-representative chat streaming workload for the Q1 2026 perf baseline & suite. Existing harness coverage is request/response (non-streaming) only, which under-measures scheduling + queueing dynamics and makes TTFT/TPOT comparisons noisy across client implementations.

What this adds: (1) A chat streaming workload generator (server SSE / chunked streaming) with configurable request-mix, sequence-length bins, and streaming cadence; (2) a concurrency ramp runner (warmup + stepped load) to mimic bursty traffic and reduce variance; (3) standardized baseline outputs for TTFT + TPOT with explicit measurement boundaries (client-side network excluded as much as practical).

Key behaviors: Request mix is modeled as weighted "cohorts" (e.g., short prompt + short decode, short prompt + long decode, medium prompt + medium decode) with configurable distributions. Concurrency ramps from N0 -> N1 -> ... with per-step dwell time, plus a warmup phase that primes model weights and KV allocations without counting metrics. Streaming pacing is implemented as a token-consumption loop that intentionally reads from the stream as fast as possible (to avoid client backpressure becoming the bottleneck). We also capture a separate "client_read_lag_ms" diagnostic to detect accidental client throttling.

Metrics: We compute TTFT as time from request send -> first token/chunk observed by client (first content token, not headers). TPOT is computed as (time from first token -> last token) / (num_tokens_after_first) and exported along with total tokens, completion time, and basic HTTP error classification. Output is emitted as JSONL (one line per request) plus an aggregated summary JSON with p50/p95/p99 for TTFT and TPOT, and throughput (req/s and output_tokens/s). The summary file is shaped to be ingested by the baseline dashboards and aligns with ADR-0172 metrics naming (full manifest wiring comes in PR-21432).

How to run: `bench run chat_streaming --endpoint $URL --model llama-3.1-70b --region us-east --mix mixes/chat_streaming_v1.json --ramp ramps/step_10min.json --seed 42 --out out/2026-01-05/`.

Notable implementation details: The generator supports both OpenAI-compatible chat completions streaming and Redwood API streaming, controlled via a `protocol` flag. We use a monotonic clock source for timing and include per-request attempt IDs for correlation with server traces. Error handling distinguishes connect timeouts, first-byte timeouts, mid-stream disconnects, and server 429/5xx to help triage overload vs regressions.

Testing: Adds unit tests for distribution sampling and pacing edge cases (empty stream, immediate EOS, chunk coalescing) and a small integration test that replays a canned streaming trace to verify TTFT/TPOT computations are stable.

title: Add canonical run manifest JSON schema + validator; ship example manifests; add CI to prevent drift
preview: Context: For the Q1'26 Perf baseline & representative workload suite we need results to be reproducible and attributable across runs, regions, GPU SKUs, and scheduler/kernel changes. Today our benchmark harnesses emit slightly different “metadata blobs”, which makes run comparison brittle and blocks automated gating. This PR introduces the canonical “run manifest” format and enforcement tooling per ADR-0172 (benchmark metrics + run manifest schema). What’s in this PR: (1) JSON Schema for the canonical run manifest (schema id + semver) covering environment capture (driver/CUDA, OS image, container digest), runtime build identifiers, model identifiers/config, scheduler config pointers, workload definition + seed, run mode (streaming/non-streaming), and required tags (region, sku, cohort). (2) A small validator CLI that can be used locally and in CI to validate manifests (and optionally “results.json” headers) against the schema. (3) Example manifests for the v1 workload shapes (chat streaming, long-prefill summarization, embeddings high-QPS) that teams can copy/paste to start running baselines. (4) CI check to prevent schema drift and to ensure any example manifests in-repo continue to validate. Design notes: The schema is intentionally strict on identity + env capture fields (to support attribution) and intentionally flexible on workload_parameters via a “workload_ref” + “params” object with a controlled allowlist (to avoid embedding sensitive prompt content). The schema includes explicit versioning (manifest_version) and supports forward-compatible additions via optional fields, but disallows unknown top-level fields to keep producers honest. Validator usage: ./tools/validate_manifest --schema schemas/run-manifest/v1/run_manifest.schema.json --manifest examples/manifests/chat_streaming_llama_70b_h100_us-east.json; CI runs this on every PR touching schemas/ or examples/. Testing: Unit tests for validator; schema tests for required/optional field behavior; golden tests for example manifests. Rollout: This is additive; existing harnesses can keep emitting old metadata while we migrate. Follow-ups (tracked in ENG-4822): wire validator into perf-canary and enforce presence of manifest for all baseline uploads; migrate legacy harnesses to emit v1 manifest sidecar.

title: introduce-cohort-emulator-with-flame-segmentation-reporter
preview: Context: We often see p95/p99 spikes in the benchmark lab but lack a compact, deterministic way to map tail episodes to kernel-level hotspots across cohorts. This PR adds a lightweight cohort emulator that replays compact multi-session cohorts with controlled timing and a new flame-segmentation reporter that emits compact 'flame segment' artifacts for downstream attribution and visualization. Motivation: Make it possible to reproduce microburst segments deterministically, compare them against a golden set, and quickly iterate on kernel/serving changes without full cluster replays. Scope: emulator core, segmentation reporter, goldset generator, CI perf job integration, docs and an example cohort. Checklist: - [x] Emulator core with deterministic seed and time dilation - [x] Flame-segmentation reporter that emits compact JSON segments and optional pprof links - [x] Goldset generator and compare tool (scripts/generate_goldset.sh) - [x] Perf CI job and threshold gating (bench/ci/perf_job.yaml) - [x] Example cohort and quickstart doc (docs/bench/cohort_emulator.md, examples/cohorts/sample_cohort.json) - [x] Unit tests and an integration perf harness (tests/integration/test_cohort_emulator.py) Limitations: This emulator is intentionally lower-fidelity than our full session replayer — it samples representative KV/cache priming and seq-length mixes rather than reproducing exact traffic. The goal is fast, repeatable reproduction of hotspot segments rather than absolute latency parity. Related: ENG-2198 (cohort diagnostics), ENG-2217 (perf regression tooling).

title: Introduce simultaneous prefetch stress scheduler and OOM spectra reporter
preview:
Motivation: We observed correlated OOMs and long-tail latency spikes when many concurrent inferences attempted identical prefix prefetching/pinning at the same time. Small-scale reproductions masked the issue because they serialized prefetch. This PR introduces a synthetic scheduler that reproduces simultaneous prefetch pressure at scale and a compact OOM 'spectra' reporter that summarizes allocation patterns leading to crashes.

What this change does: Adds a new workload runner (sim-prefetch-stressor) that can spawn thousands of virtual sessions with configurable prefetch windows, KV-cache priming patterns, and stagger jitter; the runner can optionally inject hold-until-commit semantics to simulate long-lived memory pinning. It also adds oom-spectra-reporter, a post-process tool that consumes core dumps/heap snapshots and produces an epochal histogram of allocation-site families, peak-resident working set ranges, and a small tokenized summary suitable for attaching to incidents.

Design notes: The stress scheduler is deterministic by default (seeded RNG) to allow reproducible experiments. It supports two scheduling modes: burst-synchronous (many sessions issue prefetch at the same microsecond) and windowed-overlap (configurable overlap percentage across sessions). The runner coordinates with our tracer to capture per-kernel memory high-water marks and kernel call graphs without instrumenting the runtime. The OOM spectra reporter maps back high-water marks to the synthetic workload timeline so we can attribute which phase caused the spectral spike.

How to run locally: ./bin/run-sim-prefetch --sessions 2000 --prefetch-window 128 --overlap 0.85 --hold-until-commit --trace-output /tmp/trace.pb
Then: ./tools/oom-spectra-reporter --trace /tmp/trace.pb --snapshots ./snapshots/ --out report.json

Testing and validation: The PR includes a small cluster job that runs on the benchmark lab CI cluster (k8s-ci-bench) with a 3-node rtx-cluster profile; it produces a p95/p99 latency heatmap, memory high-water timelines, and an OOM attribution CSV. We validated that burst-synchronous mode reproduces the multi-node OOM seen in PROD incident INT-842 and that moving to staggered prefetch reduced concurrent pinned bytes by ~58% in our synthetic test.

Backward compatibility: The runner and reporter are opt-in. No runtime or serving-side defaults are changed. The new tools are added under benchmark-lab tooling and do not change any public APIs.

Rollout and follow-up: After merging, we will schedule two canary runs against the perf-canary cluster (low-traffic) and integrate the OOM spectra output into incident-bot to auto-attach compact crash attribution for future incidents.

Related: ENG-1893, INT-842, previous exploratory WIP pr-314877 (context only).

title: staggered-concurrency-simulator-and-kernel-affinity-replayer
preview:
Motivation: Our existing workload runners produce steady-state and bursty traffic, but they don't model situations where multiple client cohorts arrive in staggered waves with different concurrency profiles and pinned kernel/NUMA affinities. This PR introduces a new simulator and reporting pipeline that: 1) generates multi-cohort, staggered-concurrency workloads (wave-based arrival) with configurable inter-wave gaps and tail-heavy sub-cohorts; 2) models kernel affinity and scheduler-level contention across heterogeneous serving nodes (GPU+CPU mixes) so we can attribute latency spikes to kernel-level queuing vs runtime scheduling; 3) outputs compact heatmaps and per-kernel flamepoint snapshots compatible with our telemetry exporter so downstream dashboards can surface contention hotspots and affinity misconfigurations.

What this PR changes (high level): - benchmark-lab/sim/staggered_sim.go: new wave-based workload generator with deterministic seeding and cohort config parsing. - benchmark-lab/sim/kscheduler.go: lightweight kernel-affinity emulator that can simulate pinned kernels, soft-affinity and preemptive reshuffle. - benchmark-lab/report/affinity_heatmap.py: reporting script that ingests compressed traces and emits per-kernel heatmaps and aggregated contention scores used by the perf dashboard. - benchmark-lab/harness/staggered_e2e.sh: e2e runner used in CI and perf lab runs, includes fixture priming and KV-cache warmup steps. - benchmark-lab/tests/staggered_sim_test.go and scheduler unit tests to validate determinism and distributional properties. - CI: added a perf profile job in .github/workflows/bench.yml that runs the harness in a short smoke mode.

Design notes: - The simulator supports a JSON cohort definition: arrival_rate, wave_delay_ms, concurrency_profile (p50/p95 burst multiplier), and affinity_hint (none/cpu-bound/gpu-pinned). The PR intentionally keeps the format compact and includes a compatibility shim for older YAML configs. - Kernel affinity is modeled probabilistically: we simulate the likelihood of kernel co-location and resulting queuing by tracking per-core token buckets and an adaptive wait multiplier. This keeps the runtime lightweight while producing actionable signals for the profiler. - Reporting produces two artifacts: a compressed per-epoch kernel-heatmap (suitable for long-term storage) and a short-lived flamepoint snapshot (useful for immediate triage). Both are tagged with run metadata so they can be correlated with model, sequence length distribution, and batch sizes.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests validate deterministic seeds, distribution moments (mean/variance) of inter-arrival times, and scheduler backpressure behaviors. - Smoke e2e mode (in CI) runs a single 60s wave and verifies no panics, validates output artifact schema, and uploads a small sample to a staging S3 bucket (mocked in CI). - Local performance runs (recorded in the PR comments) show a reproducible p99 spike when two GPU-pinned cohorts overlap, which matches our hypothesis about kernel co-location effects.

Migration/compatibility: Backwards compatible. Existing workload configs are accepted via the shim; new affinity hints are optional. No changes to production serving code are included here — this is an offline benchmark and reporting addition.

Checklist: - [x] Simulator implementation and unit tests - [x] Affinity scheduler emulator - [x] Reporting scripts + example output - [x] CI smoke workflow - [x] Docs (short README in sim/) - [x] Perf notes attached in the PR comments

Related work: This complements the seq-awareness and burst-runner projects (ENG-2198) by explicitly modeling kernel-level contention; it also feeds telemetry suggested by ENG-2450 for affinity-aware dashboards.

Next steps: - Wire heatmap outputs into an internal dashboard job for automated daily aggregation. - Add a targeted multi-host version that can coordinate affinity hints across machines (future PR).

title: Export benchmark results as versioned CSV/JSON artifacts for web ingestion (schema + changelog)
preview:
Context: Marketing site + interactive benchmark table needs a stable, versioned export of benchmark results that can be ingested by the web build pipeline and served via the results service (ENG-18566). This PR adds an export step that materializes benchmark outputs into (1) a canonical JSON bundle and (2) a CSV for quick inspection / spreadsheet workflows. Both artifacts are versioned, have an explicit schema version, and include a machine-readable changelog so the docs/web layer can safely detect and adapt to changes.

What’s included: - New exporter module (export/public_artifacts) that takes validated run aggregates and emits:   - artifacts/public/v1/results.json (canonical)   - artifacts/public/v1/results.csv (flat, human-friendly)   - artifacts/public/v1/changelog.json (schema + field-level changes)   - artifacts/public/v1/manifest.json (build metadata: git sha, created_at, prompt_set, regions, models, filters) - Schema versioning:   - schema_version follows semver and is embedded in every exported file header/metadata.   - Current version: 1.0.0 - JSON schema file added (schemas/public_results.schema.json) for internal validation + downstream contract tests. - Stable ordering and determinism: exporter sorts rows by (model_id, region, workload_id, context_bucket) and emits canonical JSON with stable key ordering to reduce diff noise in PRs and caching layers. - Backfill-friendly: exporter supports including a "dataset_id" and "run_group_id" so we can publish multiple refreshes without clobbering prior runs. - CLI changes: benchmark-lab runner adds --export-public-artifacts and --public-artifacts-dir flags; default path is ./artifacts/public/v1. - Tests: unit tests for schema compliance, CSV column consistency, and changelog generation; golden-file snapshot tests for results.json and results.csv. - Docs: README section on "Public artifacts" including intended consumers (docs site, marketing site, results service) and guidance for bumping schema_version.

Non-goals / guardrails: - No changes to how metrics are computed (latency/$ normalization stays in PR-4821). This is only an export + contract layer. - No PII / sensitive IDs are included; this PR intentionally only exports aggregated results and high-level config descriptors. (Redaction enforcement is handled separately; see follow-up PR-4873.)

Consumer contract (high level): - results.json includes: schema_version, dataset_id, generated_at, dimensions (model/region/workload/context_bucket), metrics (p50/p95 latency_ms, throughput_toks_per_s, cost_per_1m_tokens_usd, latency_per_dollar), and method flags (streaming=false for this dataset, caching_policy_id, batching_policy_id). - results.csv is a 1-row-per-dimension record intended for quick charting; it shares metric names with results.json where possible.

Rollout plan: - Land exporter behind the CLI flag; CI will run exports on the small smoke dataset. - Web team (Felix) will consume artifacts from a pinned schema_version and fail fast if schema_version mismatches expected major version. - Once docs site wiring is merged, we will enable exports in the full region sweep pipeline.

Risks: - Schema churn during methodology tweaks. Mitigation: semver schema_version + changelog.json + downstream contract tests. - Size of JSON bundle if we add too many slices. Mitigation: exporter supports filtering dimensions and splitting outputs per region/model in a follow-up if needed.

title: Stability gates: auto-rerun on p95 variance threshold + flaky-run CI coverage
preview: Context: We’re seeing run-to-run instability in p95 latency on some model/region pairs (queueing effects, transient noisy neighbors, kernel selection differences). This PR adds a stability gate to the benchmark pipeline that detects high variance in p95 latency and automatically re-runs the affected slice up to a bounded limit. Goal: reduce noise in published aggregates while preserving the raw evidence trail (we keep the original run results and annotate what happened). What’s included: (1) Variance detection on repeated trials for each (model, region, workload, concurrency) slice, based on p95 latency. (2) Auto rerun when variance exceeds a configurable threshold, with a cap on reruns and explicit status reporting. (3) Manifest + result artifacts include stability metadata (variance %, trials, rerun_count, stability_status, stability_notes). (4) CI coverage: simulated flaky runs with deterministic jitter to ensure the gate triggers and produces stable, explainable output. Default behavior: run N trials (default N=5) per slice; compute p95 per trial; compute variance metric = (max(p95)-min(p95))/median(p95). If variance_metric > threshold (default 0.08 = 8%), schedule rerun for that slice, up to max_reruns (default 2). If still unstable after max reruns, mark slice as UNSTABLE and keep it in output with stability_status=UNSTABLE (so downstream publish steps can choose to exclude or flag it explicitly). Config: new runner flags --stability-trials, --stability-threshold, --stability-max-reruns, --stability-disable (for local debugging). Notes: This does not change the latency/$ computation; it only governs which trials are considered stable and how reruns are executed/recorded. text: Context: We’re seeing run-to-run instability in p95 latency on some model/region pairs (queueing effects, transient noisy neighbors, kernel selection differences). This PR adds a stability gate to the benchmark pipeline that detects high variance in p95 latency and automatically re-runs the affected slice up to a bounded limit. Goal: reduce noise in published aggregates while preserving the raw evidence trail (we keep the original run results and annotate what happened). What’s included: (1) Variance detection on repeated trials for each (model, region, workload, concurrency) slice, based on p95 latency. (2) Auto rerun when variance exceeds a configurable threshold, with a cap on reruns and explicit status reporting. (3) Manifest + result artifacts include stability metadata (variance %, trials, rerun_count, stability_status, stability_notes). (4) CI coverage: simulated flaky runs with deterministic jitter to ensure the gate triggers and produces stable, explainable output. Default behavior: run N trials (default N=5) per slice; compute p95 per trial; compute variance metric = (max(p95)-min(p95))/median(p95). If variance_metric > threshold (default 0.08 = 8%), schedule rerun for that slice, up to max_reruns (default 2). If still unstable after max reruns, mark slice as UNSTABLE and keep it in output with stability_status=UNSTABLE (so downstream publish steps can choose to exclude or flag it explicitly). Config: new runner flags --stability-trials, --stability-threshold, --stability-max-reruns, --stability-disable (for local debugging). Notes: This does not change the latency/$ computation; it only governs which trials are considered stable and how reruns are executed/recorded. Follow-ups: integrate with region sweep runner to prioritize reruns in the same reservation window; wire stability_status into the publish checklist (Confluence validation checklist) so Marketing only pulls STABLE slices for public tables unless explicitly approved.

title: Publish baseline vs quant-profile cost/token + latency deltas (CSV summaries + artifacts)
preview:
Context: As part of the Quantization Profile Library rollout, we need a repeatable, reviewable set of benchmark outputs that quantify the impact of enabling a published quantization profile vs baseline precision on (a) unit economics (approx cost/token) and (b) latency/throughput across supported GPU SKUs. This PR adds a small publishing pipeline in benchmark-lab that generates normalized CSV summaries per model/profile/GPU and uploads both raw run logs and the rolled-up tables as CI artifacts. The goal is to let Applied ML + Runtime + Platform point to a single source of truth for “expected deltas” used by Console previews, rollout gates, and incident triage.

What’s included: (1) A new runner entrypoint that executes baseline + profile-enabled scenarios back-to-back with consistent warmup and pinned runtime build/commit; (2) CSV summaries under artifacts/quant_profiles/ summarizing p50/p95 end-to-end latency, decode latency, tokens/sec, VRAM peak, and derived cost/token; (3) A stable “schema_version” for the CSV format plus embedded metadata columns (model_id, variant, profile_id, profile_version, gpu_sku, runtime_commit, run_id, timestamp); (4) A README documenting how to reproduce locally and how to interpret cost/token.

Cost/token methodology: We compute cost/token as (gpu_hourly_usd / tokens_per_hour). gpu_hourly_usd comes from a static price table in this repo (documented as “estimates for comparison only”, not customer billing). tokens_per_hour is taken from measured tokens/sec after warmup and excluding initial prefill window. This is meant for relative deltas between baseline and profile-enabled on the same hardware + workload; absolute numbers will vary by fleet utilization and batching.

Workloads: This PR publishes deltas for the standard quant-profile perf suite workloads (short chat, long-context chat, and tool-call shaped prompts) at fixed concurrency points (1, 4, 16) for A100-80GB, H100-80GB, and L40S-48GB. Models currently included are the first beta targets (internal IDs): llama3.1-8b-instruct (x2), llama3.1-70b-instruct (x1), and qwen2.5-32b-instruct (x1) where profiles exist.

Example excerpt (see full CSV artifacts):
- llama3.1-8b-instruct:x2 INT8 profile (A100-80GB, conc=16, long-context): tokens/sec +22.8%, p95 e2e latency -6.1%, derived cost/token -18.6%
- llama3.1-70b-instruct:x1 FP8 mixed profile (H100-80GB, conc=4, short chat): tokens/sec +15.2%, p95 e2e latency -3.4%, derived cost/token -12.9%
- qwen2.5-32b-instruct:x1 INT8 profile (L40S-48GB, conc=16, tool-call): tokens/sec +9.7%, p95 e2e latency +2.1% (tail sensitivity), derived cost/token -7.8% (flagged for follow-up; see notes in README)

Notes / guardrails: This PR does not change any production defaults. Benchmarks are informational and intended to help validate expected savings and catch perf regressions. Profile compatibility is assumed pre-validated; this runner will fail fast if the runtime reports fallback or incompatibility (we export fallback_rate and enforce it == 0 for benchmark runs).

Follow-ups: (a) Add a lightweight “trend” job that compares latest results vs previous main commit and comments on PRs that touch runtime quantization paths; (b) Expand model coverage as new profiles are published; (c) Optionally export a JSON snapshot for Console preview ingestion once the Console team finalizes the ingestion path.

Screenshots/artifacts: CI run for this PR uploads: raw_run_logs.tgz, quant_profile_summary.csv, quant_profile_summary_by_gpu.csv, and per-model CSVs under artifacts/quant_profiles/<model_id>/.

Risk: Low. Changes are isolated to benchmark-lab scripts and CI. No customer-facing behavior change.

Refs: Confluence perf suite doc (quantization-profile-perf-benchmark-suite), runtime metrics naming from redwood/pr-18512, and rollout requirements from prd-quantization-profile-library-opt-in.
